perturbation.original_text: Aduro shall pay Consultant any amounts due that are not reasonably disputed by Aduro, by check or direct bank deposit, within thirty days after receiving the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Aduro shall pay Consultant any amounts due that are not reasonably disputed by Aduro, by check or direct bank deposit, within sixty days after receiving the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies payment within thirty days, while the modified text changes it to sixty days. This violates California Labor Code for timely payment to consultants.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 2750.5 addresses the definition of an independent contractor. While it does not explicitly define 'consultant', it emphasizes proper and timely payments as evidence for independent contractor status. Delaying payments to sixty days may be viewed negatively when determining the status of the consultant.

EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. The Contract of Employment [2750 - 2755] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2750.5. There is a rebuttable presumption affecting the burden of proof that a worker performing services for which a license is required pursuant to Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 7000) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code, or who is performing such services for a person who is required to obtain such a license is an employee rather than an independent contractor. Proof of independent contractor status includes satisfactory proof of these factors: (a) That the individual has the right to control and discretion as to the manner of performance of the contract for services in that the result of the work and not the means by which it is accomplished is the primary factor bargained for. (b) That the individual is customarily engaged in an independently established business. (c) That the individual’s independent contractor status is bona fide and not a subterfuge to avoid employee status. A bona fide independent contractor status is further evidenced by t
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment timeframe from 30 to 60 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this violates California Labor Code for timely payment to consultants and creates an inconsistency. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasize timely payments as evidence for independent contractor status under California Labor Code § 2750.5. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mandate a specific payment timeframe like 30 days, extending it to 60 days weakens the demonstration of 'bona fide independent contractor status', making the contract inconsistent with the intention of the law. The changed text sets a deadline that potentially violates state law regarding timely payments, creating an inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: From the Effective Date and for twelve (12) months after the termination of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Consultant shall not, without Aduro's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: From the Effective Date and for twenty-four (24) months after the termination of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Consultant shall not, without Aduro's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 24 months may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, which generally disfavors restraints on trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that contracts restraining a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business are void, subject to specific exceptions. While non-solicitation clauses are sometimes upheld, a 24-month restriction may be considered overly broad and thus an unreasonable restraint on trade, particularly if it hinders the consultant's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to 24 months, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Both scraped snippets state that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that a 24-month restriction may be considered overly broad and an unreasonable restraint on trade, making it unenforceable under California law. Therefore, the modification introduces a term that likely violates the law by unduly restricting the consultant's ability to work.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time on prior written notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time on prior written notice of one (1) day to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to one day may be seen as unreasonably short, particularly given the potential disruption it could cause to the consultant's other business activities. Some level of reasonable notice is often expected, especially in consulting agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2856 implies an obligation to complete undertakings, which can be interpreted to require a reasonable notice period for termination that allows for orderly transition or completion of existing work. One day may be seen as not allowing reasonable time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2856. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3. Obligations of Employee [2850 - 2866] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2856. An employee shall substantially comply with all the directions of his employer concerning the service on which he is engaged, except where such obedience is impossible or unlawful, or would impose new and unreasonable burdens upon the employee. (Enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period to one day, which the Law Explanation suggests may not allow reasonable time for orderly transition or completion of existing work, potentially contradicting an implied obligation under California Labor Code Section 2856 to complete undertakings. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing LAB 2856, states that an employee shall substantially comply with all the directions of his employer. While this snippet focuses on the employee's obligation, the Law Explanation infers a reciprocal expectation of reasonable notice to allow compliance, which the one-day notice arguably undermines. This falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.
